policy_statement: Support programs to help heavy-duty truck owners and operators transition to more efficient vehicles and cleaner fuels by 2030.
mechanism_description: Support programs help heavy-duty truck owners and operators move to more efficient vehicles and cleaner fuels, reducing freight fuel use and emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Freight transport activity → cleaner/more efficient trucks + cleaner fuels → ↓ diesel combustion (and ↓ lifecycle carbon intensity) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Heavy-duty trucking fleets are upgraded (higher efficiency powertrains, aerodynamics, electrification) and/or shifted from diesel to cleaner fuels (e.g., renewable diesel, RNG, hydrogen, electricity).  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced fossil carbon combustion per tonne-km and/or substitution with lower-carbon energy carriers reduces CO₂ emissions (and typically also reduces black carbon and some co-pollutants).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in transportation-sector GHG emissions attributable to freight operations.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from heavy-duty freight by lowering diesel consumption and/or switching to lower/zero-carbon fuels.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism primarily reduces GHG emissions by decreasing diesel use and/or reducing fuel carbon intensity in heavy-duty trucking.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While vehicle efficiency is also “resource efficiency,” the climate-relevant objective and largest effect here is emissions reduction from fuel switching and reduced combustion; thus Mitigation is dominant.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program focused only on reducing fuel use for cost/resource reasons without clear climate framing, it could be coded as Resource Efficiency; if it primarily targeted resilience (e.g., ensuring freight continuity during hazards), it could be Adaptation. Those are not the dominant effect for this mechanism as defined (“cleaner fuels” and emissions reduction).  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy that supports cleaner heavy-duty trucking via efficiency improvements and/or cleaner fuels will, by construction, reduce freight-sector GHG emissions as the main climate effect.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., electrification vs renewable diesel vs operational efficiency) depend on policy-level evidence and are not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Programs promoting “cleaner fuels” with uncertain lifecycle benefits (e.g., some biofuels) may yield smaller or variable net GHG reductions; some measures may also reduce non-CO₂ pollutants (air quality co-benefits), but the primary category remains Mitigation.
key_indicators: "Support programs"; "help heavy duty truck owners and operators transition"; "more efficient vehicles"; "cleaner fuels"; "by 2030"
co_benefits: Reduced criteria air pollutants (NOx, PM) and black carbon; potential noise reduction (if electrification); lower operating/fuel costs.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Support programs” is non-specific and could include infrastructure investment or technical assistance, but the dominant signal is an incentive/assistance approach rather than regulation or procurement.
